### Reset Consolidators

To reset a consolidator, call its reset method.

![image 350](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile350.png>)

PY

self._consolidator.reset()

If you are live trading Equities or backtesting Equities without the adjusted data normalization mode , reset your consolidators when splits and dividends occur. When a split or dividend occurs while the consolidator is in the process of building a bar, the open, high, and low may reflect prices from before the split or dividend. To avoid issues, call the consolidator's reset method and then warm it up with SCALED_RAW data from a history request .

![image 351](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile351.png>)

PY

def on_data(self, data: Slice): # When a split or dividend occurs... if (data.splits.contains_key(self._symbol) and data.splits[self._symbol].type == SplitType.SPLIT_OCCURRED or

data.dividends.contains_key(self._symbol)): # If the consolidator is working on a bar... if self._consolidator.working_data:

# Get adjusted prices for the time period of the working bar. history = self.history[TradeBar](self._symbol, self._consolidator.working_data.time, self.time,

data_normalization_mode=DataNormalizationMode.SCALED_RAW) # Reset the consolidator. self._consolidator.reset() # Warm-up the consolidator with the adjusted price data. for bar in history:

self._consolidator.update(bar)

### Examples

The following examples demonstrate common practices for implementing count consolidator.

Example 1: Tick Count Consolidator

![image 352](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile352.png>)

PY

class CountConsolidatorAlgorithm(QCAlgorithm): # Create EMA indicators for trade signal generation. _ema = ExponentialMovingAverage(14) # The _day variable controls trade daily when open. _day = -1

def initialize(self) -> None: self.set_start_date(2024, 9, 4) self.set_end_date(2024, 9, 5)

# Request SPY data to feed to indicators and trade. self.spy = self.add_equity("SPY", Resolution.TICK).symbol

# Create a 10000 trade tick consolidator to feed the "volume" bar to the EMA indicator for trend entry/exit signal. tick_consolidator = TickConsolidator(10000)

# Subscribe the consolidators to SPY data to automatically update the indicators. self.register_indicator(self.spy, self._ema, tick_consolidator)

self.set_warm_up(120)

def on_data(self, slice: Slice) -> None: ticks = slice.ticks.get(self.spy) if self._ema.is_ready and ticks and self._day != slice.time.day:

# Obtain the latest quote price from the latest tick. last_tick = sorted(ticks, key=lambda x: x.end_time, reverse=True)[0]

ema = self._ema.current.value

# Trade the trend by EMA crosses. if last_tick.price > ema and not self.portfolio[self.spy].is_long:

self.set_holdings(self.spy, 0.5) elif last_tick.price < ema and not self.portfolio[self.spy].is_short: self.set_holdings(self.spy, -0.5)

self._day = slice.time.day

Other Examples

For more examples, see the following algorithms:

Demonstration Algorithms DataConsolidationAlgorithm.py Python

Consolidating Data > Consolidator Types > Mixed-Mode Consolidators

# Consolidator Types

## Mixed-Mode Consolidators

### Introduction

Mixed-mode consolidators are a combination of count consolidators and time period consolidators. This type of consolidator aggregates n samples together or aggregates samples over a specific time period, whichever happens first.

### Consolidate Trade Bars

TradeBar consolidators aggregate TradeBar objects into TradeBar objects of the same size or larger. Follow these steps to create and manage a TradeBar consolidator based on a period of time or a number of samples, whichever occurs first:

- 1. Create the consolidator.

To create a mixed-mode consolidator, pass a timedelta object and an integer to the consolidator constructor. The consolidator time period must be greater than or equal to the resolution of the security subscription. For instance, you can aggregate minute bars into 10-minute bars, but you can't aggregate hour bars into 10-minute bars.

The time period is relative to the exchange time zone , not the algorithm time zone . If you consolidate Crypto data into daily bars, the event handler receives the consolidated bars at midnight 12:00 AM Coordinated Universal Time (UTC), regardless of the algorithm time zone.

If you consolidate on an hourly basis, the consolidator ends at the top of the hour, not every hour after the market open. For US Equities, that's 10 AM Eastern Time (ET), not 10:30 AM ET. To change this behavior, set the start_time parameter of the type constructor to timedelta( timeDeltaStartTime?>)

- 2. Add an event handler to the consolidator.

LEAN passes consolidated bars to the consolidator event handler in your algorithm. The most common error when creating consolidators is to put parenthesis () at the end of your method name when setting the event handler of the consolidator. If you use parenthesis, the method executes and the result is passed as the event handler instead of the method itself. Remember to pass the name of your method to the event system. Specifically, it should be self._consolidation_handler , not self._consolidation_handler() .

- 3. Define the consolidation handler.

The consolidation event handler receives bars when the consolidated bar closes based on the exchange time zone or the number of samples, whichever occurs first. If you subscribe to minute resolution data for Bitcoin and create an hourly consolidator, you receive consolidated bars at the top of each hour. However, if you subscribe to minute resolution data for the regular trading hours of US Equities and create a daily consolidator, you receive consolidated bars at market close or midnight, depending on your daily_precise_end_time setting .

- 4. Update the consolidator.


![image 353](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile353.png>)

PY

self._consolidator = TradeBarConsolidator(10, timedelta(days=1))

![image 354](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile354.png>)

PY

self._consolidator.data_consolidated += self._consolidation_handler

![image 355](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile355.png>)

PY

def _consolidation_handler(self, sender: object, consolidated_bar: TradeBar) -> None: pass

You can automatically or manually update the consolidator.

Automatic Updates

To automatically update a consolidator with data from the security subscription, call the add_consolidator method of the Subscription

Manager.

![image 356](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile356.png>)

PY

self.subscription_manager.add_consolidator(self._symbol, self._consolidator)

Manual Updates

Manual updates let you control when the consolidator updates and what data you use to update it. If you need to warm up a consolidator with data outside of the warm-up period , you can manually update the consolidator. To manually update a consolidator, call its update method with a TradeBar object. You can update the consolidator with data from the Slice object in the on_data method or with data from a history request .

![image 357](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile357.png>)

PY

- # Example 1: Update the consolidator with data from the Slice object def on_data(self, slice: Slice) -> None: trade_bar = slice.bars[self._symbol] self._consolidator.update(trade_bar)
- # Example 2: Update the consolidator with data from a history request history = self.history[TradeBar](self._symbol, 30, Resolution.MINUTE) for trade_bar in history:


self._consolidator.update(trade_bar)

- 5. If you create consolidators for securities in a dynamic universe and register them for automatic updates, remove the consolidator when the security leaves the universe.


![image 358](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile358.png>)

PY

self.subscription_manager.remove_consolidator(self._symbol, self._consolidator)

If you have a dynamic universe and don't remove consolidators, they compound internally, causing your algorithm to slow down and eventually die once it runs out of RAM. For an example of removing consolidators from universe subscriptions, see the GasAndCrudeOilEnergyCorrelationAlpha in the LEAN GitHub repository.

### Consolidate Quote Bars

QuoteBar consolidators aggregate QuoteBar objects into QuoteBar objects of the same size or larger. Follow these steps to create and manage a QuoteBar consolidator based on a period of time or a number of samples, whichever occurs first:

- 1. Create the consolidator.

To create a mixed-mode consolidator, pass a timedelta object and an integer to the consolidator constructor. The consolidator time period must be greater than or equal to the resolution of the security subscription. For instance, you can aggregate minute bars into 10-minute bars, but you can't aggregate hour bars into 10-minute bars.

The time period is relative to the exchange time zone , not the algorithm time zone . If you consolidate Crypto data into daily bars, the event handler receives the consolidated bars at midnight 12:00 AM Coordinated Universal Time (UTC), regardless of the algorithm time zone.

If you consolidate on an hourly basis, the consolidator ends at the top of the hour, not every hour after the market open. For US Equities, that's 10 AM Eastern Time (ET), not 10:30 AM ET. To change this behavior, set the start_time parameter of the type constructor to timedelta( timeDeltaStartTime?>)

- 2. Add an event handler to the consolidator.


![image 359](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile359.png>)

PY

self._consolidator = QuoteBarConsolidator(10, timedelta(days=1))

![image 360](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile360.png>)

LEAN passes consolidated bars to the consolidator event handler in your algorithm. The most common error when creating consolidators is to put parenthesis () at the end of your method name when setting the event handler of the consolidator. If you use parenthesis, the method executes and the result is passed as the event handler instead of the method itself. Remember to pass the name of your method to the event

system. Specifically, it should be self._consolidation_handler , not self._consolidation_handler() .

- 3. Define the consolidation handler.

The consolidation event handler receives bars when the consolidated bar closes based on the exchange time zone or the number of samples, whichever occurs first. If you subscribe to minute resolution data for Bitcoin and create an hourly consolidator, you receive consolidated bars at the top of each hour. However, if you subscribe to minute resolution data for the regular trading hours of US Equities and create a daily consolidator, you receive consolidated bars at market close or midnight, depending on your daily_precise_end_time setting .

- 4. Update the consolidator.

You can automatically or manually update the consolidator.

Automatic Updates

To automatically update a consolidator with data from the security subscription, call the add_consolidator method of the Subscription Manager.

Manual Updates

Manual updates let you control when the consolidator updates and what data you use to update it. If you need to warm up a consolidator with data outside of the warm-up period , you can manually update the consolidator. To manually update a consolidator, call its update method with a QuoteBar object. You can update the consolidator with data from the Slice object in the on_data method or with data from a history request .

- 5. If you create consolidators for securities in a dynamic universe and register them for automatic updates, remove the consolidator when the security leaves the universe.

self.subscription_manager.remove_consolidator(self._symbol, self._consolidator)

If you have a dynamic universe and don't remove consolidators, they compound internally, causing your algorithm to slow down and eventually die once it runs out of RAM. For an example of removing consolidators from universe subscriptions, see the GasAndCrudeOilEnergyCorrelationAlpha in the LEAN GitHub repository.

### Consolidate Quote Ticks

Tick quote bar consolidators aggregate Tick objects that represent quotes into QuoteBar objects. Follow these steps to create and manage a Tick quote bar consolidator based on a period of time or a number of samples, whichever occurs first:

- 1. Create the consolidator.

To create a mixed-mode consolidator, pass a timedelta object and an integer to the consolidator constructor. The consolidator time period must be greater than or equal to the resolution of the security subscription. For instance, you can aggregate minute bars into 10-minute bars, but you can't aggregate hour bars into 10-minute bars.

The time period is relative to the exchange time zone , not the algorithm time zone . If you consolidate Crypto data into daily bars, the event handler receives the consolidated bars at midnight 12:00 AM Coordinated Universal Time (UTC), regardless of the algorithm time zone.

If you consolidate on an hourly basis, the consolidator ends at the top of the hour, not every hour after the market open. For US Equities, that's 10 AM Eastern Time (ET), not 10:30 AM ET. To change this behavior, set the start_time parameter of the type constructor to timedelta( timeDeltaStartTime?>)

- 2. Add an event handler to the consolidator.

LEAN passes consolidated bars to the consolidator event handler in your algorithm. The most common error when creating consolidators is to put parenthesis () at the end of your method name when setting the event handler of the consolidator. If you use parenthesis, the method executes and the result is passed as the event handler instead of the method itself. Remember to pass the name of your method to the event system. Specifically, it should be self._consolidation_handler , not self._consolidation_handler() .

- 3. Define the consolidation handler.

The consolidation event handler receives bars when the consolidated bar closes based on the exchange time zone or the number of samples, whichever occurs first. If you subscribe to tick resolution data for Bitcoin and create an hourly consolidator, you receive consolidated bars at the top of each hour. However, if you subscribe to tick resolution data for the regular trading hours of US Equities and create a daily consolidator, you receive consolidated bars at market close or midnight, depending on your daily_precise_end_time setting .

- 4. Update the consolidator.


![image 371](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile371.png>)

PY

self._consolidator = TickQuoteBarConsolidator(10, timedelta(milliseconds=100))

![image 372](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile372.png>)

PY

self._consolidator.data_consolidated += self._consolidation_handler

![image 373](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile373.png>)

PY

def _consolidation_handler(self, sender: object, consolidated_bar: QuoteBar) -> None: pass

You can automatically or manually update the consolidator.

Automatic Updates

To automatically update a consolidator with data from the security subscription, call the add_consolidator method of the Subscription Manager.

![image 374](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile374.png>)

PY

self.subscription_manager.add_consolidator(self._symbol, self._consolidator)

Manual Updates

Manual updates let you control when the consolidator updates and what data you use to update it. If you need to warm up a consolidator with data outside of the warm-up period , you can manually update the consolidator. To manually update a consolidator, call its update method with a Tick object. You can update the consolidator with data from the Slice object in the on_data method or with data from a history request .

![image 375](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile375.png>)

PY

- # Example 1: Update the consolidator with data from the Slice object def on_data(self, slice: Slice) -> None:

ticks = slice.ticks[self._symbol] for tick in ticks:

self._consolidator.update(tick)

- # Example 2: Update the consolidator with data from a history request ticks = self.history[Tick](self._symbol, timedelta(minutes=3), Resolution.TICK) for tick in ticks:


self._consolidator.update(tick)

- 5. If you create consolidators for securities in a dynamic universe and register them for automatic updates, remove the consolidator when the security leaves the universe.


![image 376](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile376.png>)

PY

self.subscription_manager.remove_consolidator(self._symbol, self._consolidator)

If you have a dynamic universe and don't remove consolidators, they compound internally, causing your algorithm to slow down and eventually die once it runs out of RAM. For an example of removing consolidators from universe subscriptions, see the GasAndCrudeOilEnergyCorrelationAlpha in the LEAN GitHub repository.

### Consolidate Other Data

<dataType> consolidators aggregate various types of data objects into <dataType> objects of the same size or larger. If you consolidate custom data or alternative datasets, check the definition of the data class to see its data type. Follow these steps to create and manage a <dataType> consolidator based on a period of time or a number of samples, whichever occurs first:

- 1. Create the consolidator.

To create a mixed-mode consolidator, pass a timedelta object and an integer to the consolidator constructor. The consolidator time period must be greater than or equal to the resolution of the security subscription. For instance, you can aggregate minute bars into 10-minute bars, but you can't aggregate hour bars into 10-minute bars.

The time period is relative to the exchange time zone , not the algorithm time zone . If you consolidate Crypto data into daily bars, the event handler receives the consolidated bars at midnight 12:00 AM Coordinated Universal Time (UTC), regardless of the algorithm time zone.

If you consolidate on an hourly basis, the consolidator ends at the top of the hour, not every hour after the market open. For US Equities, that's 10 AM Eastern Time (ET), not 10:30 AM ET. To change this behavior, set the start_time parameter of the type constructor to timedelta( timeDeltaStartTime?>)

- 2. Add an event handler to the consolidator.


![image 377](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile377.png>)

PY

self._consolidator = DynamicDataConsolidator(10, timedelta(days=1))

![image 378](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile378.png>)

PY

self._consolidator.data_consolidated += self._consolidation_handler

LEAN passes consolidated bars to the consolidator event handler in your algorithm. The most common error when creating consolidators is to put parenthesis () at the end of your method name when setting the event handler of the consolidator. If you use parenthesis, the method executes and the result is passed as the event handler instead of the method itself. Remember to pass the name of your method to the event

system. Specifically, it should be self._consolidation_handler , not self._consolidation_handler() .

- 3. Define the consolidation handler.

The consolidation event handler receives bars when the consolidated bar closes based on the exchange time zone or the number of samples, whichever occurs first.

- 4. Update the consolidator.

You can automatically or manually update the consolidator.

Automatic Updates

To automatically update a consolidator with data from the data subscription, call the add_consolidator method of the Subscription Manager.

Manual Updates

Manual updates let you control when the consolidator updates and what data you use to update it. If you need to warm up a consolidator with data outside of the warm-up period , you can manually update the consolidator. To manually update a consolidator, call its update method with a <dataType> object. You can update the consolidator with data from the Slice object in the on_data method or with data from a history request .

- 5. If you create consolidators for securities in a dynamic universe and register them for automatic updates, remove the consolidator when the security leaves the universe.


![image 379](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile379.png>)

PY

def _consolidation_handler(self, sender: object, consolidated_bar: <dataType>) -> None: pass

![image 380](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile380.png>)

PY

self.subscription_manager.add_consolidator(self._symbol, self._consolidator)

![image 381](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile381.png>)

PY

# Example 1: Update the consolidator with data from the Slice object def on_data(self, slice: Slice) -> None:

if self._symbol in slice: self._consolidator.update(slice[self._symbol])

# Example 2: Update the consolidator with data from a history request history = self.history[<dataType>](self._symbol, 30, Resolution.DAILY) for data in history:

self._consolidator.update(data)

![image 382](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile382.png>)

PY

self.subscription_manager.remove_consolidator(self._symbol, self._consolidator)

If you have a dynamic universe and don't remove consolidators, they compound internally, causing your algorithm to slow down and eventually die once it runs out of RAM. For an example of removing consolidators from universe subscriptions, see the GasAndCrudeOilEnergyCorrelationAlpha in the LEAN GitHub repository.

### Reset Consolidators

To reset a consolidator, call its reset method.

![image 383](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile383.png>)

PY

self._consolidator.reset()

If you are live trading Equities or backtesting Equities without the adjusted data normalization mode , reset your consolidators when splits and dividends occur. When a split or dividend occurs while the consolidator is in the process of building a bar, the open, high, and low may reflect prices from before the split or dividend. To avoid issues, call the consolidator's reset method and then warm it up with SCALED_RAW data from a history request .

![image 384](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile384.png>)

PY

def on_data(self, data: Slice): # When a split or dividend occurs... if (data.splits.contains_key(self._symbol) and data.splits[self._symbol].type == SplitType.SPLIT_OCCURRED or

data.dividends.contains_key(self._symbol)): # If the consolidator is working on a bar... if self._consolidator.working_data:

# Get adjusted prices for the time period of the working bar. history = self.history[TradeBar](self._symbol, self._consolidator.working_data.time, self.time,

data_normalization_mode=DataNormalizationMode.SCALED_RAW) # Reset the consolidator. self._consolidator.reset() # Warm-up the consolidator with the adjusted price data. for bar in history:

self._consolidator.update(bar)

### Examples

The following examples demonstrate common practices for implementing mixed-mode consolidators.

Example 1: Opening Range Breakout

The following algorithm demonstrates an opening range breakout strategy for BJK (a Gaming ETF). We create a 15-count/30-minute trade bar as the opening range for signal generation. If the close price at 10 am is higher than the high price of the aggregated opening bar, we long BJK and exit by a 2% trailing stop loss.

![image 385](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile385.png>)

PY

class MixedModeConsolidatorAlgorithm(QCAlgorithm): _high_price = 0

def initialize(self) -> None:

self.set_start_date(2024, 9, 1) self.set_end_date(2024, 12, 31)

# Request CARZ data to feed to the consolidators and trade. self._equity = self.add_equity("CARZ", Resolution.MINUTE).symbol

# Create a 10-count-or-20-minute trade bar consolidator to create the opening range for trade signal generation. # CARZ is a less liquid ETF, we use a mixed-mode consolidator. self._consolidator = TradeBarConsolidator(10, timedelta(minutes=20))

# Subscribe the consolidators to CARZ data for automatic updating. self.subscription_manager.add_consolidator(self._equity, self._consolidator)

# We check if the entry condition is attained 30 minutes after the market opens. self.schedule.on(

self.date_rules.every_day(self._equity), self.time_rules.after_market_open(self._equity, 30), self.entry

)

self.set_warm_up(1)

def entry(self) -> None: # Check if the entry condition is met: the current price is higher than the high price of the open range. current = self.securities[self._equity].close if current > self._consolidator.consolidated.high:

self.market_order(self._equity, 20) # Cache the current price for trailing stop loss need. self._high_price = current

def on_data(self, slice: Slice) -> None: bar = slice.bars.get(self._equity) if bar and self.portfolio.invested:

# Update the high price if the current high is higher to update the trailing stop price. if bar.high > self._high_price:

self._high_price = bar.high

# Exit position if the trailing stop loss is hit. if bar.close < self._high_price * 0.98:

self.liquidate() self._high_price = 0

Consolidating Data > Consolidator Types > Renko Consolidators

# Consolidator Types

## Renko Consolidators

Consolidating Data > Consolidator Types > Renko Consolidators > Renko Consolidators

# Renko Consolidators

## Renko Consolidators

### Introduction

Most Renko consolidators aggregate bars based on a fixed price movement. The RenkoConsolidator produces Renko bars by their traditional definition. In the case of a $1 bar size, the RenkoConsolidator produces bars that have a body spanning 1. The opening price of the first bar is set to the closest 1 multiple of the first trade. When the price moves by at least 1, the first bar closes. If a bar is a rising bar, the following bar closes when the price moves 1 above the closing price of the previous bar or1 below the opening price of the previous bar. If a bar is a falling bar, the following bar closes when the price moves 1 below the closing price of the previous bar or $1 above the opening price of the previous bar. If the price jumps multiple dollars in a single tick, the RenkoConsolidator produces multiple $1 bars in a single time step.

![image 386](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile386.png>)

### Consolidate Trade Bars

TradeBar consolidators aggregate TradeBar objects into RenkoBar objects. Follow these steps to create and manage a TradeBar consolidator based on the traditional Renko bar rules:

- 1. Create the consolidator.

To create a Renko consolidator, pass the bar size to the RenkoConsolidator constructor.

- 2. Add an event handler to the consolidator.

LEAN passes consolidated bars to the consolidator event handler in your algorithm. The most common error when creating consolidators is to put parenthesis () at the end of your method name when setting the event handler of the consolidator. If you use parenthesis, the method executes and the result is passed as the event handler instead of the method itself. Remember to pass the name of your method to the event system. Specifically, it should be self._consolidation_handler , not self._consolidation_handler() .

- 3. Define the consolidation handler.


![image 387](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile387.png>)

PY

# Create a Renko consolidator that emits a bar when the price moves $1 self._consolidator = RenkoConsolidator(1)

![image 388](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile388.png>)

PY

self._consolidator.data_consolidated += self._consolidation_handler

def _consolidation_handler(self, sender: object, consolidated_bar: RenkoBar) -> None: pass

The consolidation event handler receives bars when the price movement forms a new Renko bar.

- 4. Update the consolidator.

You can automatically or manually update the consolidator.

Automatic Updates

To automatically update a consolidator with data from the security subscription, call the add_consolidator method of the Subscription Manager.

Manual Updates

Manual updates let you control when the consolidator updates and what data you use to update it. If you need to warm up a consolidator with data outside of the warm-up period , you can manually update the consolidator. To manually update a consolidator, call its update method with a TradeBar object. You can update the consolidator with data from the Slice object in the on_data method or with data from a history request .

- 5. If you create consolidators for securities in a dynamic universe and register them for automatic updates, remove the consolidator when the security leaves the universe.


![image 390](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile390.png>)

PY

self.subscription_manager.add_consolidator(self._symbol, self._consolidator)

![image 391](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile391.png>)

PY

- # Example 1: Update the consolidator with data from the Slice object def on_data(self, slice: Slice) -> None:

trade_bar = slice.bars[self._symbol] self._consolidator.update(trade_bar)

- # Example 2: Update the consolidator with data from a history request history = self.history[TradeBar](self._symbol, 30, Resolution.MINUTE) for trade_bar in history:


self._consolidator.update(trade_bar)

![image 392](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile392.png>)

PY

self.subscription_manager.remove_consolidator(self._symbol, self._consolidator)

If you have a dynamic universe and don't remove consolidators, they compound internally, causing your algorithm to slow down and eventually die once it runs out of RAM. For an example of removing consolidators from universe subscriptions, see the GasAndCrudeOilEnergyCorrelationAlpha in the LEAN GitHub repository.

### Reset Consolidators

To reset a consolidator, call its reset method.

![image 411](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile411.png>)

PY

self._consolidator.reset()

If you are live trading Equities or backtesting Equities without the adjusted data normalization mode , reset your consolidators when splits and dividends occur. When a split or dividend occurs while the consolidator is in the process of building a bar, the open, high, and low may reflect prices from before the split or dividend. To avoid issues, call the consolidator's reset method and then warm it up with SCALED_RAW data from a history request .

![image 412](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile412.png>)

PY

def on_data(self, data: Slice): # When a split or dividend occurs... if (data.splits.contains_key(self._symbol) and data.splits[self._symbol].type == SplitType.SPLIT_OCCURRED or

data.dividends.contains_key(self._symbol)): # If the consolidator is working on a bar... if self._consolidator.working_data:

# Get adjusted prices for the time period of the working bar. history = self.history[TradeBar](self._symbol, self._consolidator.working_data.time, self.time,

data_normalization_mode=DataNormalizationMode.SCALED_RAW) # Reset the consolidator. self._consolidator.reset() # Warm-up the consolidator with the adjusted price data. for bar in history:

self._consolidator.update(bar)

Consolidating Data > Consolidator Types > Renko Consolidators > Classic Renko Consolidators

# Renko Consolidators

## Classic Renko Consolidators

### Introduction

Most Renko consolidators aggregate bars based on a fixed price movement. The ClassicRenkoConsolidator produces a different type of Renko bars than the RenkoConsolidator . A ClassicRenkoConsolidator with a bar size of 1 produces a new bar that spans1 every time an asset closes $1 away from the close of the previous bar. If the price jumps multiple dollars in a single tick, the ClassicRenkoConsolidator only produces one bar per time step where the open of each bar matches the close of the previous bar.

### Consolidate Trade Bars

TradeBar consolidators aggregate TradeBar objects into RenkoBar objects. Follow these steps to create and manage a TradeBar consolidator based on the preceding Renko bar rules:

- 1. Create the consolidator.

To create a classic Renko consolidator, pass the bar size to the ClassicRenkoConsolidator constructor.

The ClassicRenkoConsolidator has the following default behavior:

It uses the value property of the IBaseData object it receives to build the Renko bars

It ignores the volume of the input data

It enforces the open and close of each bar to be a multiple of the bar size

To build the Renko bars with a different property than the value of the IBaseData object, provide a selector argument. The selector should be a function that receives the IBaseData object and returns a decimal value.

To add a non-zero volume property to the Renko bars, provide a volume_selector argument. The volume_selector should be a function that receives the IBaseData object and returns a decimal value.

To relax the requirement that the open and close of the Renko bars must be a multiple of bar size, disable the even_bars argument. If you disable even_bars , the open value of the first Renko bar is set to the first value from the selector . The following opening and closing Renko bar values are all multiples of the first value from the selector

- 2. Add an event handler to the consolidator.


![image 413](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile413.png>)

PY

# Create a Classic Renko consolidator that emits a bar when the price moves $1 self._consolidator = ClassicRenkoConsolidator(1)

![image 414](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile414.png>)

PY

self._consolidator = ClassicRenkoConsolidator(1, selector = lambda data: data.high)

![image 415](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile415.png>)

PY

self._consolidator = ClassicRenkoConsolidator(1, volume_selector = lambda data: data.volume)

![image 416](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile416.png>)

PY

self._consolidator = ClassicRenkoConsolidator(1, even_bars=False)

![image 417](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile417.png>)

PY

self._consolidator.data_consolidated += self._consolidation_handler

LEAN passes consolidated bars to the consolidator event handler in your algorithm. The most common error when creating consolidators is to put parenthesis () at the end of your method name when setting the event handler of the consolidator. If you use parenthesis, the method

executes and the result is passed as the event handler instead of the method itself. Remember to pass the name of your method to the event system. Specifically, it should be self._consolidation_handler , not self._consolidation_handler() .

- 3. Define the consolidation handler.

The consolidation event handler receives bars when the price movement forms a new classic Renko bar.

- 4. Update the consolidator.

You can automatically or manually update the consolidator.

Automatic Updates

To automatically update a consolidator with data from the security subscription, call the add_consolidator method of the Subscription Manager.

Manual Updates

Manual updates let you control when the consolidator updates and what data you use to update it. If you need to warm up a consolidator with data outside of the warm-up period , you can manually update the consolidator. To manually update a consolidator, call its update method with a TradeBar object. You can update the consolidator with data from the Slice object in the on_data method or with data from a history request .

- 5. If you create consolidators for securities in a dynamic universe and register them for automatic updates, remove the consolidator when the security leaves the universe.


![image 418](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile418.png>)

PY

def _consolidation_handler(self, sender: object, consolidated_bar: RenkoBar) -> None: pass

![image 419](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile419.png>)

PY

self.subscription_manager.add_consolidator(self._symbol, self._consolidator)

![image 420](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile420.png>)

PY

- # Example 1: Update the consolidator with data from the Slice object def on_data(self, slice: Slice) -> None: trade_bar = slice.bars[self._symbol] self._consolidator.update(trade_bar)
- # Example 2: Update the consolidator with data from a history request history = self.history[TradeBar](self._symbol, 30, Resolution.MINUTE) for trade_bar in history:


self._consolidator.update(trade_bar)

![image 421](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile421.png>)

PY

self.subscription_manager.remove_consolidator(self._symbol, self._consolidator)

If you have a dynamic universe and don't remove consolidators, they compound internally, causing your algorithm to slow down and eventually die once it runs out of RAM. For an example of removing consolidators from universe subscriptions, see the GasAndCrudeOilEnergyCorrelationAlpha in the LEAN GitHub repository.

### Consolidate Quote Bars

QuoteBar consolidators aggregate QuoteBar objects into RenkoBar objects. Follow these steps to create and manage a QuoteBar consolidator based on the preceding Renko bar rules:

- 1. Create the consolidator.


To create a classic Renko consolidator, pass the bar size to the ClassicRenkoConsolidator constructor.

![image 422](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile422.png>)

PY

# Create a Classic Renko consolidator that emits a bar when the price moves $1 self._consolidator = ClassicRenkoConsolidator(1)

The ClassicRenkoConsolidator has the following default behavior:

It uses the value property of the IBaseData object it receives to build the Renko bars

It ignores the volume of the input data

It enforces the open and close of each bar to be a multiple of the bar size

The following arguments enable you to create Renko bars that aggregate the excess liquidity on the bid.

![image 423](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile423.png>)

PY

self._consolidator = ClassicRenkoConsolidator(10, lambda data: data.value, lambda data: data.last_bid_size - data.last_ask_size)

To relax the requirement that the open and close of the Renko bars must be a multiple of bar size, disable the even_bars argument. If you disable even_bars , the open value of the first Renko bar is set to the first value from the selector . The following opening and closing Renko bar values are all multiples of the first value from the selector

![image 424](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile424.png>)

PY

self._consolidator = ClassicRenkoConsolidator(1, even_bars=False)

- 2. Add an event handler to the consolidator.

LEAN passes consolidated bars to the consolidator event handler in your algorithm. The most common error when creating consolidators is to put parenthesis () at the end of your method name when setting the event handler of the consolidator. If you use parenthesis, the method executes and the result is passed as the event handler instead of the method itself. Remember to pass the name of your method to the event system. Specifically, it should be self._consolidation_handler , not self._consolidation_handler() .

- 3. Define the consolidation handler.

The consolidation event handler receives bars when the price movement forms a new classic Renko bar.

- 4. Update the consolidator.


![image 425](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile425.png>)

PY

self._consolidator.data_consolidated += self._consolidation_handler

![image 426](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile426.png>)

PY

def _consolidation_handler(self, sender: object, consolidated_bar: RenkoBar) -> None: pass

You can automatically or manually update the consolidator.

Automatic Updates

To automatically update a consolidator with data from the security subscription, call the add_consolidator method of the Subscription Manager.

![image 427](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile427.png>)

PY

self.subscription_manager.add_consolidator(self._symbol, self._consolidator)

Manual Updates

Manual updates let you control when the consolidator updates and what data you use to update it. If you need to warm up a consolidator with data outside of the warm-up period , you can manually update the consolidator. To manually update a consolidator, call its update method with a QuoteBar object. You can update the consolidator with data from the Slice object in the on_data method or with data from a history request .

![image 428](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile428.png>)

PY

- # Example 1: Update the consolidator with data from the Slice object def on_data(self, slice: Slice) -> None:

quote_bar = slice.quote_bars[self._symbol] self._consolidator.update(quote_bar)

- # Example 2: Update the consolidator with data from a history request history = self.history[QuoteBar](self._symbol, 30, Resolution.MINUTE) for quote_bar in history:


self._consolidator.update(quote_bar)

- 5. If you create consolidators for securities in a dynamic universe and register them for automatic updates, remove the consolidator when the security leaves the universe.


![image 429](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile429.png>)

PY

self.subscription_manager.remove_consolidator(self._symbol, self._consolidator)

If you have a dynamic universe and don't remove consolidators, they compound internally, causing your algorithm to slow down and eventually die once it runs out of RAM. For an example of removing consolidators from universe subscriptions, see the GasAndCrudeOilEnergyCorrelationAlpha in the LEAN GitHub repository.

### Consolidate Trade Ticks

Tick consolidators aggregate Tick objects into RenkoBar objects. Follow these steps to create and manage a Tick consolidator based on the preceding Renko bar rules:

- 1. Create the consolidator.

To create a classic Renko consolidator, pass the bar size to the ClassicRenkoConsolidator constructor.

The ClassicRenkoConsolidator has the following default behavior:

It uses the value property of the IBaseData object it receives to build the Renko bars

It ignores the volume of the input data

It enforces the open and close of each bar to be a multiple of the bar size

To relax the requirement that the open and close of the Renko bars must be a multiple of bar size, disable the even_bars argument. If you disable even_bars , the open value of the first Renko bar is set to the first value from the selector . The following opening and closing Renko bar values are all multiples of the first value from the selector

- 2. Add an event handler to the consolidator.

LEAN passes consolidated bars to the consolidator event handler in your algorithm. The most common error when creating consolidators is to put parenthesis () at the end of your method name when setting the event handler of the consolidator. If you use parenthesis, the method executes and the result is passed as the event handler instead of the method itself. Remember to pass the name of your method to the event system. Specifically, it should be self._consolidation_handler , not self._consolidation_handler() .

- 3. Define the consolidation handler.


![image 430](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile430.png>)

Automatic Updates

Manual Updates

You can update the consolidator with data from the Slice object in the on_data method or with data from a history request .

ticks = slice.ticks[self._symbol] for tick in ticks:

self._consolidator.update(tick)

### Reset Consolidators

To reset a consolidator, call its reset method.

![image 444](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile444.png>)

PY

self._consolidator.reset()

If you are live trading Equities or backtesting Equities without the adjusted data normalization mode , reset your consolidators when splits and dividends occur. When a split or dividend occurs while the consolidator is in the process of building a bar, the open, high, and low may reflect prices from before the split or dividend. To avoid issues, call the consolidator's reset method and then warm it up with SCALED_RAW data from a history request .

![image 445](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile445.png>)

PY

def on_data(self, data: Slice): # When a split or dividend occurs... if (data.splits.contains_key(self._symbol) and data.splits[self._symbol].type == SplitType.SPLIT_OCCURRED or

data.dividends.contains_key(self._symbol)): # If the consolidator is working on a bar... if self._consolidator.working_data:

# Get adjusted prices for the time period of the working bar. history = self.history[TradeBar](self._symbol, self._consolidator.working_data.time, self.time,

data_normalization_mode=DataNormalizationMode.SCALED_RAW) # Reset the consolidator. self._consolidator.reset() # Warm-up the consolidator with the adjusted price data. for bar in history:

self._consolidator.update(bar)

### Examples

The following examples demonstrate some common practices for implementing classic renko consolidator.

Example 1: 10 Dollar Bar Range SMA Cross

The following algorithm creates a dollar bar for SPY. Then, for each 10-dollar bar, we find their range (high - low) and feed to an SMA indicator, such that we obtain a trend indicator of the range of SPY. We trade VXX as the proxy of the SPY volatility based on the trend of the range SMA.

![image 446](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile446.png>)

PY

class ClassicRenkoBarConsolidatorAlgorithm(QCAlgorithm): _high = 0 _low = 10000 _count = 0 _sma = SimpleMovingAverage(10)

def initialize(self) -> None:

self.set_start_date(2024, 9, 1) self.set_end_date(2024, 12, 31)

# Request SPY data to feed the consolidators and indicators for trade signal generation. self._spy = self.add_equity("SPY", Resolution.MINUTE).symbol # Request VXX data for trading. self._vxx = self.add_equity("VXX", Resolution.MINUTE).symbol

# Create a dollar bar of SPY to track the volatility. self._consolidator = ClassicRenkoConsolidator(1) self._consolidator.data_consolidated += self.on_consolidated

# Subscribe the consolidators to SPY data for automatic updating. self.subscription_manager.add_consolidator(self._spy, self._consolidator)

self._sma.window.size = 2; self.set_warm_up(100)

def on_consolidated(self, sender: object, bar: TradeBar) -> None: # Update the SPY range of the current batch. self._high = max(self._high, bar.high) self._low = min(self._low, bar.low) self._count += 1

# Update SMA of SPY range of the last 10 Renko bars. if self._count >= 10:

self._sma.update(bar.end_time, self._high - self._low)

# Reset high and low. self._high = 0 self._low = 10000 self._count = 0

def on_data(self, slice: Slice) -> None:

if self._sma.is_ready: current = self._sma.current.value previous = self._sma.previous.value

# If the range trend increases, buy VXX as a proxy for the increasing volatility. if current > previous and not self.portfolio[self._vxx].is_long:

self.set_holdings(self._vxx, 0.5) # If the range trend decreases, sell VXX as a proxy for decreasing volatility. elif current < previous and not self.portfolio[self._vxx].is_short:

self.set_holdings(self._vxx, -0.5)

Other Examples

For more examples, see the following algorithms:

Demonstration Algorithms ClassicRenkoConsolidatorAlgorithm.py Python

Consolidating Data > Consolidator Types > Renko Consolidators > Volume Renko Consolidators

# Renko Consolidators

## Volume Renko Consolidators

### Introduction

Volume Renko consolidators aggregate bars based on a fixed trading volume. These types of bars are commonly known as volume bars. A VolumeRenkoConsolidator with a bar size of 10,000 produces a new bar every time 10,000 units of the security trades in the market. If the trading volume of a single time step exceeds two step sizes (i.e. >20,000), the VolumeRenkoConsolidator produces multiple bars in a single time step.

### Consolidate Trade Bars

TradeBar consolidators aggregate TradeBar objects into VolumeRenkoBar objects. Follow these steps to create and manage a TradeBar consolidator based on custom set volume traded:

1. Create the consolidator.

To create a Volume Renko consolidator, pass the bar size to the VolumeRenkoConsolidator constructor.

![image 447](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile447.png>)

PY

# Create a Volume Renko consolidator that emits a bar every time 10,000 units trade self._consolidator = VolumeRenkoConsolidator(10000)

2. Add an event handler to the consolidator.

![image 448](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile448.png>)

PY

self._consolidator.data_consolidated += self._consolidation_handler

LEAN passes consolidated bars to the consolidator event handler in your algorithm. The most common error when creating consolidators is to put parenthesis () at the end of your method name when setting the event handler of the consolidator. If you use parenthesis, the method executes and the result is passed as the event handler instead of the method itself. Remember to pass the name of your method to the event system. Specifically, it should be self._consolidation_handler , not self._consolidation_handler() .

3. Define the consolidation handler.

![image 449](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile449.png>)

PY

def _consolidation_handler(self, sender: object, consolidated_bar: VolumeRenkoBar) -> None: pass

The consolidation event handler receives bars when the trading volume forms a new Volume Renko bar.

4. Update the consolidator.

You can automatically or manually update the consolidator.

Automatic Updates

To automatically update a consolidator with data from the security subscription, call the add_consolidator method of the Subscription Manager.

![image 450](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile450.png>)

PY

self.subscription_manager.add_consolidator(self._symbol, self._consolidator)

Manual Updates

Manual updates let you control when the consolidator updates and what data you use to update it. If you need to warm up a consolidator with data outside of the warm-up period , you can manually update the consolidator. To manually update a consolidator, call its update method with a TradeBar object. You can update the consolidator with data from the Slice object in the on_data method or with data from a history request .

![image 451](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile451.png>)

PY

- # Example 1: Update the consolidator with data from the Slice object def on_data(self, slice: Slice) -> None: trade_bar = slice.bars[self._symbol] self._consolidator.update(trade_bar)
- # Example 2: Update the consolidator with data from a history request history = self.history[TradeBar](self._symbol, 30, Resolution.MINUTE) for trade_bar in history:


self._consolidator.update(trade_bar)

5. If you create consolidators for securities in a dynamic universe and register them for automatic updates, remove the consolidator when the security leaves the universe.

![image 452](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile452.png>)

PY

self.subscription_manager.remove_consolidator(self._symbol, self._consolidator)

If you have a dynamic universe and don't remove consolidators, they compound internally, causing your algorithm to slow down and eventually die once it runs out of RAM. For an example of removing consolidators from universe subscriptions, see the GasAndCrudeOilEnergyCorrelationAlpha in the LEAN GitHub repository.

self._consolidator.update(tick)

### Reset Consolidators

To reset a consolidator, call its reset method.

![image 459](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile459.png>)

PY

self._consolidator.reset()

If you are live trading Equities or backtesting Equities without the adjusted data normalization mode , reset your consolidators when splits and dividends occur. When a split or dividend occurs while the consolidator is in the process of building a bar, the open, high, and low may reflect prices from before the split or dividend. To avoid issues, call the consolidator's reset method and then warm it up with SCALED_RAW data from a history request .

![image 460](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile460.png>)

PY

def on_data(self, data: Slice): # When a split or dividend occurs... if (data.splits.contains_key(self._symbol) and data.splits[self._symbol].type == SplitType.SPLIT_OCCURRED or

data.dividends.contains_key(self._symbol)): # If the consolidator is working on a bar... if self._consolidator.working_data:

# Get adjusted prices for the time period of the working bar. history = self.history[TradeBar](self._symbol, self._consolidator.working_data.time, self.time,

data_normalization_mode=DataNormalizationMode.SCALED_RAW) # Reset the consolidator. self._consolidator.reset() # Warm-up the consolidator with the adjusted price data. for bar in history:

self._consolidator.update(bar)

### Examples

The following examples demonstrate common practices for implementing volume renko consolidator.

Example 1: Bollinger Bands From Volume Renko Bar

The following algorithm uses 100,000-share volume Renko bars to spot mean reversal opportunities on SPY with Bollinger Bands. Equal volume bars could provide more equal information in each bar, so the standard deviation and trade opportunities are more credible.

![image 461](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile461.png>)

PY

class VolumeRenkoBarConsolidatorAlgorithm(QCAlgorithm): # We use a Bollinger band to signal mean reversal opportunities. _bbands = BollingerBands(20, 2, MovingAverageType.EXPONENTIAL)

def initialize(self) -> None:

self.set_start_date(2024, 9, 1) self.set_end_date(2024, 12, 31)

# Request SPY data to feed for the consolidators, indicators, and trade signal generation. self._spy = self.add_equity("SPY", Resolution.MINUTE).symbol

# Create a 100,000-share bar of SPY to obtain a more equally-informed bar. self._consolidator = VolumeRenkoConsolidator(100000)

# Subscribe the consolidators to SPY data to automatically update the indicator. self.register_indicator(self._spy, self._bbands, self._consolidator)

# Warm up the indicator for immediate use. self.set_warm_up(20)

def on_data(self, slice: Slice) -> None: bar = slice.bars.get(self._spy) if not self.is_warming_up else None if self._bbands.is_ready and bar:

upper_band = self._bbands.upper_band.current.value middle_band = self._bbands.middle_band.current.value lower_band = self._bbands.lower_band.current.value

# Trade the mean reversal by short-selling SPY if the current price exceeds the upper threshold. if bar.close > upper_band and not self.portfolio[self._spy].is_long:

self.set_holdings(self._spy, -0.5) # Trade the mean reversal by buying SPY if the price is below the lower threshold. elif bar.close < lower_band and not self.portfolio[self._spy].is_short:

self.set_holdings(self._spy, 0.5) # Exit position if the price is mean-reverted that crosses the EMA reference. elif (self.portfolio[self._spy].quantity > 0 and bar.close > middle_band)\ or (self.portfolio[self._spy].quantity < 0 and bar.close < middle_band):

self.liquidate(self._spy)

Other Examples

For more examples, see the following algorithms:

Demonstration Algorithms VolumeRenkoConsolidatorAlgorithm.py Python

Consolidating Data > Consolidator Types > Range Consolidators

# Consolidator Types

## Range Consolidators

Consolidating Data > Consolidator Types > Range Consolidators > Range Consolidators

# Range Consolidators

## Range Consolidators

### Introduction

Most Range consolidators aggregate bars based on a fixed price movement. It is very much like a Renko Bar, but instead of consolidating a fix movement from the opening price, a Range Bar would fix the range of the whole bar, i.e. High - Low. A new bar is formed when the range of the whole bar reached a preset value, thus it completely neglects the time effect and focuses on the variance/information carried by the price movement.

![image 462](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile462.png>)

### Consolidate Trade Bars

TradeBar consolidators aggregate TradeBar objects into RangeBar objects. Follow these steps to create and manage a TradeBar consolidator based on the preset range of price movement:

- 1. Create the consolidator.

To create a Range consolidator, pass the bar size to the RangeConsolidator constructor.

- 2. Add an event handler to the consolidator.

LEAN passes consolidated bars to the consolidator event handler in your algorithm. The most common error when creating consolidators is to put parenthesis () at the end of your method name when setting the event handler of the consolidator. If you use parenthesis, the method executes and the result is passed as the event handler instead of the method itself. Remember to pass the name of your method to the event system. Specifically, it should be self._consolidation_handler , not self._consolidation_handler() .

- 3. Define the consolidation handler.


![image 463](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile463.png>)

PY

# Create a Range consolidator that emits a bar when the high and low differs $1 self._consolidator = RangeConsolidator(1)

![image 464](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile464.png>)

PY

self._consolidator.data_consolidated += self._consolidation_handler

def _consolidation_handler(self, sender: object, consolidated_bar: RangeBar) -> None: pass

The consolidation event handler receives bars when the price movement forms a new Range bar.

- 4. Update the consolidator.

You can automatically or manually update the consolidator.

Automatic Updates

To automatically update a consolidator with data from the security subscription, call the add_consolidator method of the Subscription Manager.

Manual Updates

Manual updates let you control when the consolidator updates and what data you use to update it. If you need to warm up a consolidator with data outside of the warm-up period , you can manually update the consolidator. To manually update a consolidator, call its update method with a TradeBar object. You can update the consolidator with data from the Slice object in the on_data method or with data from a history request .

- 5. If you create consolidators for securities in a dynamic universe and register them for automatic updates, remove the consolidator when the security leaves the universe.


![image 466](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile466.png>)

PY

self.subscription_manager.add_consolidator(self._symbol, self._consolidator)

![image 467](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile467.png>)

PY

- # Example 1: Update the consolidator with data from the Slice object def on_data(self, slice: Slice) -> None: trade_bar = slice.bars[self._symbol] self._consolidator.update(trade_bar)
- # Example 2: Update the consolidator with data from a history request history = self.history[TradeBar](self._symbol, 30, Resolution.MINUTE) for trade_bar in history:


self._consolidator.update(trade_bar)

![image 468](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile468.png>)

PY

self.subscription_manager.remove_consolidator(self._symbol, self._consolidator)

If you have a dynamic universe and don't remove consolidators, they compound internally, causing your algorithm to slow down and eventually die once it runs out of RAM. For an example of removing consolidators from universe subscriptions, see the GasAndCrudeOilEnergyCorrelationAlpha in the LEAN GitHub repository.

### Consolidate Quote Bars

QuoteBar consolidators aggregate QuoteBar objects into RangeBar objects. Follow these steps to create and manage a QuoteBar consolidator based on the preset range of price movement:

1. Create the consolidator.

To create a Range consolidator, pass the bar size to the RangeConsolidator constructor.

![image 469](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile469.png>)

PY

# Create a Range consolidator that emits a bar when the high and low differs $1 self._consolidator = RangeConsolidator(1)

2. Add an event handler to the consolidator.

![image 470](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile470.png>)

PY

self._consolidator.data_consolidated += self._consolidation_handler

LEAN passes consolidated bars to the consolidator event handler in your algorithm. The most common error when creating consolidators is to put parenthesis () at the end of your method name when setting the event handler of the consolidator. If you use parenthesis, the method executes and the result is passed as the event handler instead of the method itself. Remember to pass the name of your method to the event system. Specifically, it should be self._consolidation_handler , not self._consolidation_handler() .

3. Define the consolidation handler.

![image 471](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile471.png>)

PY

def _consolidation_handler(self, sender: object, consolidated_bar: RangeBar) -> None: pass

The consolidation event handler receives bars when the price movement forms a new Range bar.

4. Update the consolidator.

You can automatically or manually update the consolidator.

Automatic Updates

To automatically update a consolidator with data from the security subscription, call the add_consolidator method of the Subscription Manager.

![image 472](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile472.png>)

PY

self.subscription_manager.add_consolidator(self._symbol, self._consolidator)

Manual Updates

Manual updates let you control when the consolidator updates and what data you use to update it. If you need to warm up a consolidator with data outside of the warm-up period , you can manually update the consolidator. To manually update a consolidator, call its update method with a QuoteBar object. You can update the consolidator with data from the Slice object in the on_data method or with data from a history request .

![image 473](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile473.png>)

PY

# Example 1: Update the consolidator with data from the Slice object def on_data(self, slice: Slice) -> None:

quote_bar = slice.quote_bars[self._symbol] self._consolidator.update(quote_bar)

# Example 2: Update the consolidator with data from a history request history = self.history[QuoteBar](self._symbol, 30, Resolution.MINUTE) for quote_bar in history:

self._consolidator.update(quote_bar)

5. If you create consolidators for securities in a dynamic universe and register them for automatic updates, remove the consolidator when the security leaves the universe.

![image 474](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile474.png>)

PY

self.subscription_manager.remove_consolidator(self._symbol, self._consolidator)

If you have a dynamic universe and don't remove consolidators, they compound internally, causing your algorithm to slow down and eventually die once it runs out of RAM. For an example of removing consolidators from universe subscriptions, see the GasAndCrudeOilEnergyCorrelationAlpha in the LEAN GitHub repository.

### Consolidate Trade Ticks

Tick consolidators aggregate Tick objects into RangeBar objects. Follow these steps to create and manage a Tick consolidator based on the preset range of price movement:

1. Create the consolidator.

To create a Range consolidator, pass the bar size to the RangeConsolidator constructor.

# Create a Range consolidator that emits a bar when the high and low differs $1 self._consolidator = RangeConsolidator(1)

2. Add an event handler to the consolidator.

![image 476](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile476.png>)

PY

self._consolidator.data_consolidated += self._consolidation_handler

LEAN passes consolidated bars to the consolidator event handler in your algorithm. The most common error when creating consolidators is to put parenthesis () at the end of your method name when setting the event handler of the consolidator. If you use parenthesis, the method executes and the result is passed as the event handler instead of the method itself. Remember to pass the name of your method to the event system. Specifically, it should be self._consolidation_handler , not self._consolidation_handler() .

3. Define the consolidation handler.

![image 477](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile477.png>)

PY

def _consolidation_handler(self, sender: object, consolidated_bar: RangeBar) -> None: pass

The consolidation event handler receives bars when the price movement forms a new Range bar.

4. Update the consolidator.

You can automatically or manually update the consolidator.

Automatic Updates

To automatically update a consolidator with data from the security subscription, call the add_consolidator method of the Subscription Manager.

![image 478](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile478.png>)

PY

self.subscription_manager.add_consolidator(self._symbol, self._consolidator)

Manual Updates

Manual updates let you control when the consolidator updates and what data you use to update it. If you need to warm up a consolidator with data outside of the warm-up period , you can manually update the consolidator. To manually update a consolidator, call its update method with a Tick object. You can update the consolidator with data from the Slice object in the on_data method or with data from a history request .

![image 479](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile479.png>)

PY

# Example 1: Update the consolidator with data from the Slice object def on_data(self, slice: Slice) -> None:

ticks = slice.ticks[self._symbol] for tick in ticks:

self._consolidator.update(tick)

# Example 2: Update the consolidator with data from a history request ticks = self.history[Tick](self._symbol, timedelta(minutes=3), Resolution.TICK) for tick in ticks:

self._consolidator.update(tick)

5. If you create consolidators for securities in a dynamic universe and register them for automatic updates, remove the consolidator when the security leaves the universe.

![image 480](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile480.png>)

PY

self.subscription_manager.remove_consolidator(self._symbol, self._consolidator)

If you have a dynamic universe and don't remove consolidators, they compound internally, causing your algorithm to slow down and eventually die once it runs out of RAM. For an example of removing consolidators from universe subscriptions, see the GasAndCrudeOilEnergyCorrelationAlpha in the LEAN GitHub repository.

Tick quote bar consolidators aggregate Tick objects that represent quotes into RangeBar objects. Follow these steps to create and manage a Tick quote bar consolidator based on the preset range of price movement:

1. Create the consolidator.

![image 481](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile481.png>)

LEAN passes consolidated bars to the consolidator event handler in your algorithm. The most common error when creating consolidators is to put parenthesis () at the end of your method name when setting the event handler of the consolidator. If you use parenthesis, the method executes and the result is passed as the event handler instead of the method itself. Remember to pass the name of your method to the event system. Specifically, it should be self._consolidation_handler , not self._consolidation_handler() .

3. Define the consolidation handler.

![image 483](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile483.png>)

PY

def _consolidation_handler(self, sender: object, consolidated_bar: RangeBar) -> None: pass

The consolidation event handler receives bars when the price movement forms a new Range bar.

Automatic Updates

Manual Updates

Manual updates let you control when the consolidator updates and what data you use to update it. If you need to warm up a consolidator with data outside of the warm-up period , you can manually update the consolidator. To manually update a consolidator, call its update method with a Tick object. You can update the consolidator with data from the Slice object in the on_data method or with data from a history request .

![image 485](<Quantconnect-Writing-Algorithms-Python_part2_images/imageFile485.png>)

PY

# Example 1: Update the consolidator with data from the Slice object def on_data(self, slice: Slice) -> None:

ticks = slice.ticks[self._symbol] for tick in ticks:

self._consolidator.update(tick)

# Example 2: Update the consolidator with data from a history request ticks = self.history[Tick](self._symbol, timedelta(minutes=3), Resolution.TICK) for tick in ticks:

5. If you create consolidators for securities in a dynamic universe and register them for automatic updates, remove the consolidator when the security leaves the universe.

If you have a dynamic universe and don't remove consolidators, they compound internally, causing your algorithm to slow down and eventually die once it runs out of RAM.
